coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots/bisulfite assisted chemiluminescence of rhodamine B-H2O2 system for sensitive recognition of HCHO
coredata.dc:creator: Khataee, Alireza
Hassanzadeh, Javad
Lotfi, Roya
Joo, Sang Woo
coredata.dc:description: A novel and powerful chemiluminescence (CL) system was developed based on the promoting effect of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/bisulfite on rhodamine B (RB)-H2O2 CL reaction. GQDs were synthesized by a simple, green and affordable approach and characterized using their distinctive absorption and emission patterns. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) transmission and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis were implemented for complementary studies on the structural and morphological characteristics of GQDs. The simultaneous enhancing effect of the synthesized GQDs and bisulfite on the CL emission of RB-H2O2 reaction provided a more strong and efficient CL system. The mechanism of CL emission was described. Furthermore, HCHO as a well-known pollutant, could selectively diminish the developed CL system. This effect was linearly proportional to HCHO concentration which turned the introduced CL system to a susceptible chemosensor for HCHO. Under the optimized operational condition, the linear changing of CL response was obtained in HCHO concentration ranges of 0.02–5μgL−1 and 5–11μgL−1, with limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of 6ngL−1 and 20ngL−1, respectively. Ultimately, presented CL chemosensor was served as an accurate tool for determination of formaldehyde concentration in water and wastewater samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bisulfite
Chemiluminescence
Graphene quantum dots
Flow injection
Formaldehyde

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemiluminescence reaction sensitized by GQDs and NaHSO3

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the University of Tabriz for the support provided. We also acknowledge the support of Iran Science Elites Federation. This work is funded by the grant NRF-2015-002423 of the National Research Foundation of Korea.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic image of HCHO quenching effect on the CL emission of RB-H2O2-GQDs-Bisulfite system.

Schematic diagram of flow-injection CL system; (a): NaOH solution; (b): Mixture of rhodamine B, GQDs, SDS and NaHSO3 with or without HCHO sample or standard solution (c): H2O as the carrier; (d): H2O2 solution; M: mixing tube; V: injection valve; F: flow cell; W: waste; D: detector (photomultiplier tube); and R: recorder (computer).
(a): XRD pattern; (b): FT-IR spectrum; (c): TEM and (d):HRTEM images for the synthesized GQDs; The inset of (c) shows particle size distribution of GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra for the synthesized GQDs (a: 0.48, b: 0.8, 1.5mmolL−1); and (b) fluorescence emission with different excitation wavelength (a-g: 300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420) for the synthesized GQDs (0.05mmolL−1).

Kinetic curves for (a): rhodamine B-H2O2; (b): rhodamine B-H2O2-GQDs; (c): rhodamine B-H2O2-GQDs-SDS; (d): rhodamine B-H2O2-GQDs-SDS-NaHSO3 CL system; and (e): rhodamine B-H2O2-GQDs-SDS-NaHSO3 CL system in the presence of HCHO. The concentrations of H2O2, rhodamine B, NaOH, GQDs, SDS and HCHO were 0.8molL−1, 0.02mmolL−1, 0.8molL−1, 5.2mmolL−1, 0.15mmolL−1 and 3.0μgL−1, respectively.

CL spectra of (A) rhodamine B-SDS-H2O2 CL system in basic media in the absence or presence of GQDs and HSO3 −, and (B) GQDs-SDS-H2O2 CL system in basic media in the absence or presence of HSO3 −, [the spectra were recorded by continuous flow of chemicals; (A): H2O2 (1mmolL−1) in one line and NaOH (0.8molL−1), SDS (0.3mmolL−1) and RB (0.05mmolL−1) with or without GQDs (0.01molL−1) and HSO3 − (0.3mmolL−1) in the other line, (B): H2O2 (3mmolL−1) in one line and NaOH (0.6molL−1), SDS (0.5mmolL−1) and GQDs (0.03molL−1) with or without HSO3 − (1mmolL−1) in the other line].
a) Fluorescence spectra of TA (0.2mmmolL−1)-H2O2 (1mmolL−1) solution (pH 11 adjusted by NaOH) in the absence (a) and presence of 0.2mmolL−1 NaHSO3 (b), 5mmolL−1 GQDs (c) and NaHSO3/GQDs (d).

a) Time profiles of rhodamine B-H2O2-GQDs-SDS-NaHSO3 CL system in the presence of different concentrations of HCHO (μgL−1) and, b) corresponding calibration curve for the determination of HCHO in optimum condition.
A comparison of different analytical techniques for the determination of HCHO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: SERS: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, NPs: Nanoparticles, RGO: Reduced graphene oxide, QDs: Quantum dots, CL: Chemiluminescence.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tolerable concentration ratios with respect to 8.0μgL−1 of HCHO.

Acetaldehyde, Propionaldehyde, Butyraldehyde, Benzaldehyde, Formic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Obtained results for the determination of HCHO in spiked water samples by developed CL method.
Obtained results for the determination of HCHO in spiked water samples by developed CL method and comparision with an official (fluorimetric) method.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots/bisulfite assisted chemiluminescence of rhodamine B-H2O2 system for sensitive recognition of HCHO

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 51666-16471 Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Nanotechnology Engineering, Near East University, 99138 Nicosia, North Cyprus, Mersin 10, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Nanotechnology Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Yeungnam University, 712-749 Gyeongsan, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 51666-16471 Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and powerful chemiluminescence (CL) system was developed based on the promoting effect of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/bisulfite on rhodamine B (RB)-H2O2 CL reaction. GQDs were synthesized by a simple, green and affordable approach and characterized using their distinctive absorption and emission patterns. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) transmission and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis were implemented for complementary studies on the structural and morphological characteristics of GQDs. The simultaneous enhancing effect of the synthesized GQDs and bisulfite on the CL emission of RB-H2O2 reaction provided a more strong and efficient CL system. The mechanism of CL emission was described. Furthermore, HCHO as a well-known pollutant, could selectively diminish the developed CL system. This effect was linearly proportional to HCHO concentration which turned the introduced CL system to a susceptible chemosensor for HCHO. Under the optimized operational condition, the linear changing of CL response was obtained in HCHO concentration ranges of 0.02–5μgL−1 and 5–11μgL−1, with limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of 6ngL−1 and 20ngL−1, respectively. Ultimately, presented CL chemosensor was served as an accurate tool for determination of formaldehyde concentration in water and wastewater samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL), as an electromagnetic emission through a chemical reaction, have been extensively accepted as a potent analytical device with a large diversity of advantages such as the ease of operation, simple and inexpensive instrumentation with a very low detection limit and excellent sensitivity [1]. In recent decades, nanomaterials have been effectively exploited as an active unit in various CL systems due to their unique physicochemical attributes [1–3]. Several researchers have reported the positive effect of various nanostructures on CL reactions that reveal the high potential of nano-assisted CL methods for analytical purpose [1–4]. In reported systems, the studied nanomaterials acted as the emitting luminophore, catalyst and/or energy acceptor.

Carbonaceous compounds, as an old companion of human kind, have been attracted the attention of the researchers for using them to develop modern science and technology [5]. In the last two decades, we witnessed the discovery of the carbon-based nanomaterial (CBN). Graphene, an important subclass of CBN, has two-dimensional (2D) structure which consists of a single sheet of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb configuration [5–9]. The impressive characteristics of low toxicity, excellent chemical and thermal stability, high flexibility and unique electrical and optical properties of graphene has captivated the scientists’ consideration [5,9–11]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are small fractions of graphene with the sizes of less than 100nm. Conversion of two-dimension graphene sheets to zero-dimension GQDs results in development of quantum confinement and edge effects following presence of band gap in GQDs [12–16]. Consequently, GQDs show potent photoluminescence properties which extend their range of applicability. Moreover, the layer structure of graphene is maintained in GQDs, thus, they have higher surface area with a better surface grafting features compared to other carbon-based nanostructures. GQDs display great water solubility, low toxicity, outstanding stability, and desirable biocompatibility [12,15,17–19]. So, it is expected that the application of GQDs in CL analytical methods provide many important benefits. From the reports, GQDs participate in CL systems as emitting species, catalysts or cause an efficient CL resonance energy transfer. For example, Al-Ogaidi et al. [20] have evaluated the participation of GQDs as energy acceptors in a CL resonance energy transfer process and have introduced an immunoassay for ovarian cancer biomarker CA-125. In another study, Amjadi et al. [18,21,22] reported some sensitive CL analytical application of GQDs based on their catalytic and emitting activities.

Formaldehyde (HCHO) belongs to the aldehyde class with a characteristic pungent odor which is a well-known toxic compound. It has serious hazardous effect on human health, since, it stimulates critical cells of human body like nervous and sight systems and also brings about insomnia, nausea, headaches, and allergic skin reactions [23–27]. The certain characteristics of HCHO such as preservation, disinfectant and bleaching have paved the way for implementation of this organic compound in several industrial sectors, such as food, coating, adhesive and cosmetics [27]. On the other hand, automobile exhaust gases and photo-oxidation of hydrocarbons are key sources of HCHO that result in its existence in the atmosphere which cause atmospheric deposition and aquatic environment contamination [28]. International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the US National Toxicology Program, and the Occupational Safety and Health Association (OSHA) evaluated the cancer risk of HCHO on human and categorized this compound as a carcinogenic [25]. Contamination of aquatic environment with HCHO remains a challenge for analytical researches to construct a novel technique for simple and accurate determination of this compound. Various procedures have been introduced for this purpose, including fluorimetry [29–33], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [26,34,35], chromatography [23,25], spectrophotometry [36–39], and chemiluminescence (CL) [28,40–43]. Among these procedures, CL technique causes great sensation, so that several valuable surveys have been triggered for implementation of CL as a promising procedure in important fields [44].

Here, a strong CL system based on rhodamine B (RB) oxidation was introduced as a high efficient and sensitive chemosensor for HCHO. GQDs were synthesized via a simple and green approach and it is indicated that prepared GQDs have a remarkable promoting effect on the chemiluminescence emission produced by RB-H2O2 reaction. Subsequent investigations revealed that the presence of sodium dodecyl sulfonate (SDS) and sodium bisulfite lead to a greater enhancement effect of GQDs. Furthermore, it is interestingly observed that HCHO manifested diminishing effect on the resulted RB-H2O2-GQDs-SDS-NaHSO3 CL system. The quenching influence of HCHO on the RB-H2O2-GQDs-SDS-NaHSO3 caused a considerable sensation for employing of the aforementioned CL technique for HCHO determination (Scheme 1 ).

Most of the chemicals including glucose, rhodamine B (RB), sodium bisulfite, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), etc. were of analytical grade and purchased from Merck Co. (Germany) and employed without further purifying process. Deionized water was consumed throughout the experiments. Stock standard solution of HCHO (50mgL−1) was provided by dilution of 125μL of 37% HCHO solution using deionized water.

The CL responses of the associated reactions were received through a FB12 luminometer (Berthold detection systems, Germany) as emission detector and a connected computer for data evaluations. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were collected by an UV–vis spectrophotometer (S2000, WPA Lightwave, England). The crystal phase (crystalline phase configuration) of the prepared GQDs was assessed via the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum achieved from Siemens X-ray powder diffractometer (D5000, Siemens, Germany) occupying Cu Kα as the exciting source (λ=1.54056Å) at room temperature. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) appraisal was accomplished on a IR-spectrometer (Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany). Moreover, the morphological assays of GQDs were conducted by the participation of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image gained from Cs-corrected TEM (JEM-2200FS, JEOL, Japan) acting at 200kV. For recording CL spectra, spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu RF-5301, Japan) without excitation source was employed.

Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic arrangement of different divisions of the employed CL system in flow mode. This apparatus was composed of four channels constructed by polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) tubes with internal diameter (i.d.) of 1.0mm (NaOH solution in line a, the mixture of RB, GQDs, SDS and NaHSO3 with or without HCHO standard or sample solutions in line b, deionized water as carrier in line c, and H2O2 solution as oxidant in the line d). The carrier stream was carrying the mixed output of the streams concerning to line (a) and (b), combined by Y-pieces. This was conducted via a six-port valve possessing 150μL loop. CL emission was triggered after the inserting of the mixed streams to the flow cell and combining with H2O2 solution, which was placed in front of the detector. The mentioned structure was equipped with software provided by the Berthold Company to process the CL emission. The determination of HCHO was accomplished regarding to the quenching impact of HCHO on the CL which was calculated using the formula: ΔI=I0 −Is, where I0 and Is are representative of CL responses in the absence and presence of HCHO, respectively.

GQDs were synthesized by glucose as a the main precursor [18]. Briefly, 6.0g of pure glucose powder was melted. The heating was continued to complete the polymerization process of glucose molecules leading to the formation of a clear and orange color solution. This color change of the solution was an indicator for the GQDs production. 25mL of deionized water was added to the resultant liquid with vigorous stirring. It is worthy to note that the resultant GQDs can be kept in the refrigerator at 4°C for a long time without any changes. Moreover, the sizes of the synthesized GQDs could be controlled by filtration (10,000 and 8000 NMWL) after centrifuging at 10,000rpm for 20min.

Subsequently, the fresh samples of the tap water, underground water and wastewater were gathered into polyethylene flasks pursuant to the standard methods. The dispersed solid particles were separated via passing through a polyamide membrane filter with the pore size of 0.45μm. The tap and underground waters samples were provided by spiking certain amounts of the HCHO standard solution. The samples were then delivered through a packed exchange column containing the cation-exchanging resin (Chelex 100) possessing flow rate of 4mLmin−1 to prevent the probable interfering effect of metallic ions on the determination procedure. Finally, the CL responses for samples were collected from the employed CL were applied for the determination of HCHO concentrations. These processes can be done online by using a flow through system using a 0.45μm cartridge followed by a packed exchange column before the CL flow system. The offered system is simple and needs no excess time for pretreatment purpose.

XRD pattern of the as-formed GQDs is depicted in Fig. 2 a. The XRD diffraction peaks of the GQDs is located at 2θ (diffraction angle) values between 10° and 25°, representing close compliance with (002) plane reflections, and can be assigned to the graphite structure. Moreover, broad peak in the XRD pattern of GQDs is representative of the small-sized QDs [19]. Correspondingly, the interlayer space of the synthesized QDs was assessed to be 4.05Å which matched well with the values obtained for the GQDs synthesized by different procedures [16]. The enhanced interlayer distance of GQDs in comparison with the bulk graphite (3.34Å) [11,45], was induced from the introducing of oxygen-containing functional groups such as −OH, −COOH and −COR which resulted in the broadened peak of the XRD pattern [11,19]. On the other hand, the average crystal size of synthesized GQDs was calculated to be about 5.2nm by Debye–Scherrer equation, using the highest XRD peak. The aforementioned functional groups were also observed in the FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs (Fig. 2b). The peaks located near 1643 and 1789cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CC and CO, respectively. Moreover, the wide peak around 3200–3600cm−1 could be attributed to stretching vibrations of OH group. The peaks located at 1364cm−1 and 2929cm−1 were related to the bending and stretching vibrations of CH, respectively. On the other hand, the peak at about 1035cm−1 confirmed the presence of CO bonds in GQDs structure [18].

The morphology and size of the synthesized GQDs were appraised using TEM image, as represented in Fig. 2c. From this image, the QDs were almost mono dispersed spherical nanoparticles. The results of size distribution of the prepared GQDs are shown in inset of Fig. 2c. The major diameter distribution of the GQDS ranged from 1 to 13nm with an average value of 5.6nm with is in good agreement with XRD results. Also, high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image from synthesized GQDs (Fig. 2d) indicated obvious crystal lattice with the lattice parameter of 0.24nm matching with the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene. After the size separation of GQDs using filtration method, the size distribution of two separated GQDs solution were obtained in the ranges of 1–6.2nm and 5.3–12.5nm, respectively.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs of different concentrations are displayed in Fig. 3 a. It is clear that the QDs possess two characteristic absorbance situated at the areas of about 225nm and 280nm. These peaks could be related to the well-known π–π* transitions of CC groups and n–π* transitions of CO bonds, respectively [18]. Furthermore, a strong fluorescence emission relevant to the excitation wavelength was obtained for GQDs (Fig. 3b) [18]. The emission spectra was gradually moved to high wavelengths by increasing excitation wavelength. This phenomenon is a characteristic feature of GQDs. The maximum emission intensity was observed at about 450nm (with an excitation wavelength of 360nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemiluminescence reaction sensitized by GQDs and NaHSO3
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic profiles of the established CL system related to the RB-H2O2 reaction in the presence of GQDs, SDS and NaHSO3 are displayed in Fig. 4 . The curve-a in Fig. 4 describes a weak emission generated during the reaction of basic RB and H2O2 in a continuous flow system. The experiments indicated that GQDs considerably intensified the CL emission of RB-H2O2 reaction (Fig. 4, curve b). Moreover, it was observed that SDS promoted the generated CL signal over again (Fig. 4, curve c). More interestingly, bisulfite improved the intensifying effect of GQDs and made a more amplified CL emission. The enhancing effect of GQDs improved more than 3 times in the presence of bisulfite (Fig. 4, curve d). Accordingly, a more intensive CL emission was created based on RB-GQDs-bisulfite-SDS-H2O2 system. The CL reaction was also examined in the presence of glucose, as the employed precursor of GQDs synthesis. The results showed no sensible effect which showed that the enhancing effect was assignable to the synthesized GQDs. The developed CL system with a high emission intensity could be useful for the analysis purposes. It was found in this study that HCHO could quench the induced CL system (curve e of Fig. 4). Besides, it should be mentioned that the effects of some other aldehydes such as acetaldehyde, glutaraldehyde, propionaldehyde, butyraldehyde, benzaldehyde and glyoxal on the RB-H2O2-GQDs-SDS-NaHSO3 were also investigated. The effects of these compounds were ignorable and the system had an acceptable selectivity for HCHO.

To achieve the best analytical responses, the influence of the associated parameters namely concentrations of RB, H2O2, NaOH, GQDs, SDS and NaHSO3 were assessed. In this regard, responses of the proposed CL reactions involving various concentrations of RB in the range of 0.001–0.03mmolL−1 were monitored, (see Fig. S1a). According to the figure, the strongest CL response was achieved at 0.02mmolL−1 of RB. Higher concentrations of RB decreased the CL responses which could be ascribed to the self-absorption of the excessive RB [46]. Therefore, 0.02mmolL−1 was recognized as the optimized concentration of RB.

Hydrogen peroxide, as the only oxidant in the present investigation, has an essential role in the CL reaction. Hence, the effect of H2O2 was assessed for varying concentrations from 0.1 to 1.6molL−1 and represented in Fig. S1b. Regarding to this figure, increasing trend in the CL responses was observed up to 0.8molL−1 of H2O2 and then, CL emissions decreased. Accordingly, 0.8molL−1 of H2O2 was used as optimum value during the experiments.

In order to assess the effect of GQDs, various concentrations of GQDs in the range of 0.16–8mmolL−1 (concentration of GQDs was assigned to the number of C atoms in glucose which was used in a certain amount) were applied and the results were shown in Fig. S1c. The results revealed that the increasing in the concentration of GQDs up to 5.2mmolL−1 amplified the CL response; whereas, higher concentrations had impractical effect. Therefore, this amount was selected as optimum concentration. Furthermore, the CL signals were recorded at different concentrations of NaOH ranging from 0.01 to 2.0molL−1 (Fig. S1d). According to the results, the strongest CL response was generated at 0.8molL−1 of NaOH and this value was selected for further evaluations.

This is well known that some surfactants act as sensitizer and show a positive effect on the most of CL systems. So, two surfactants, namely: SDS, and CTAB, were examined in different concentrations for developed CL reaction. It was observed that SDS had more improving effect than CTAB, probably due to the greater electrostatic interactions. The investigation of Zeta potential for the solution containing GQDs showed a change from −11.3mV to −25.7mV in the presence of SDS. This could verify their electrostatic interactions. The effect of SDS in various concentrations of SDS (0.005–0.3mmolL−1) revealed that the appropriate CL response was achieved at 0.15mmolL−1 (Fig. S1e). The excessive concentrations of SDS decreased the CL response that could be assigned to the formation of micelles [47]. Ultimately, evaluations were conducted to optimize the NaHSO3 concentration. The changes in the CL response were followed by varying concentrations of NaHSO3 in the range of 0.005–1mmolL−1 (Fig. S1f). From the figure, 0.2mmolL−1 of NaHSO3 was chosen as optimum concentration due to the generation of higher CL response under this concentration.

The size effect of GQDs on their enhancing activity on the CL emission was investigated using two different size ranges of GQDs separated by filtration method (below 5nm and over 5nm). The results showed that the size separation process decreased the catalytic action of GQDs. Therefore, the as-prepared GQDs was applied to catalyze the CL system, without any size filtration process.

Indeed, the identification of the emitting species in the CL reactions could be useful for interpretation of a possible and rational reaction pathway. On this regard, fluorescence and UV–vis patterns can provide helpful information. The CL spectra which were implemented to recognize the participated species in the reactions, is shown in Fig. 5 . The characteristic emission through the CL reaction of RB and H2O2 appeared as a distinct peak situated near 580nm (spectrum a in Fig. 5a) which could be assigned to the RB molecules. This is because there was a good agreement between the obtained CL spectrum and inherent fluorescence emission of RB (λem =585nm and λex =555nm). Also, the existence of −OH radicals was confirmed by means of terephthalic acid (TA), as a familiar −OH indicator. The reaction of non-fluorescent TA with intermediate hydroxyl radicals produces a strongly fluorescent product, namely 2-hydroxyterephthalicacid (HTA) (Fig. 6 ). On the other hand, a set of experiments were conducted in the presence of hydroxyl radical scavengers, such as dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and mannitol. Results showed that the increase in the concentration of radical scavengers caused a diminishing effect on the CL responses, in which the CL intensity of about 75% was quenched in the presence of 1% DMSO or 0.1mmolL−1 of mannitol. Accordingly, it can be concluded that OH was the main intermediate species which were resulted from the decomposition of H2O2 (Eq. (1)). These radicals reacted with RB and produced oxidized RB in an excited state (RBox
*) (Eq. (2)), which could act as initial emitting species (Eq. (3)) [48,49]. The weak emission peak at about 430nm (spectrum a in Fig. 5a) in the obtained CL spectrum could be for RBox
* [50,51]. On the other hand, RBox
* can transfer the energy to RB molecules which caused the main emission peak at 580nm (Eqs. 4).
(1) H2O2 →2 OH
(2)
OH+RB→RBox *+OH−
(3) RBox
* →RBox
+hυ (430nm)
(4a) RBox
* +RB→RBox
+RB*
(4b) RB* →RB+hυ (580nm)

Peroxidase activity of graphene based nanostructures has been frequently reported in the literature. Therefore, GQDs could serve as a catalytic center to accelerate the decomposition of H2O2 that lead to an increase in the number of OH radicals (Eq. (5)). Consequently, the rate of RB-H2O2 reaction increased and the CL intensity enhanced. This process was confirmed by the relative increase in the intensity of the peak at 430nm, which is correspond to RBox
* (spectrum b in Fig. 5a). Moreover, in the presence of GQDs, one more peak at about 470nm was also detected that could be assigned to GQDs at excited state (GQDs*). This could be generated through two probable mechanisms: (i) the reaction of OH radicals with GQDs and/or (ii) energy transfer from RBox
* species to GQDs. It should be noted that H2O2 could react with OH− in alkaline condition and generated HO2 − and H2O, according to Eq. (6a). Besides, superoxide anion (O2 −) could be developed through the interaction of OH and HO2 −, as shown in Eq. (6b). Furthermore, the induced O2 − species could have an interaction with OH and produce the singlet oxygen (1O2), as shown in Eq. (6c). The induced 1O2 species could react with each other and generate excited dimer (O2)2 * (Eq. (6d)) [52,53]. The produced OH and O2 − radicals could react with GQDs by hole–electron injection generating GQDs +and GQDs − (Eq. (7)a, b) which lead to GQDs* species (Eq. (7c)). Next, the energetic dimer ((O2)2 *) and GQDs could participate in CL resonance energy transfer as an energy donor and acceptor, giving rise to the formation of GQDs* (Eq. (8)) [54]. Ultimately, reforming the ground state of GQDs can emanate emissions around 460nm (Eq. (9)).
(5) H2O2 +GQDs (catalyst) → 2 OH
(6a) OH− +H2O2 →HO2 − +H2O
(6b) 2 OH+HO2 − →2 O2 − +H+
(6c)
O2−+ OH → OH− +1O2
(6d)
1O2 +1O2 → (O2)2 * (6d)
(7a) GQDs+ OH→GQDs + +OH−
(7b) GQDs+ O2 − →GQDs − +O2
(7c) GQDs + +GQDs − →GQDs*
(8) (O2)2 * +GQDs→GQDs* +2O2
(9) GQDs* →GQDs+hυ (460nm)

On the other hand, in the presence of both HSO3 − and GQDs, the CL spectrum (curve c of Fig. 5a) revealed that the characteristic peaks were located at the same wavelength with increased intensities. So, it could be considered that the emitting species were the same. The increasing effect of bisulfite could be explained as follows: The reaction between HSO3 − and H2O2 formed the HOOSO2 − (Eq. (10)a) which then decomposed to SO3 − and OH radicals (Eq. (10)b) [55,56]. The generated OH and SO3 − species reacted with GQDs and generate GQDs + and GQDs
− , respectively (Eqs. 11a, b), which in turn generated the GQDs* (Eq. (7c)) [54].
(10a) HSO3 − +H2O2 →HOOSO2 − +H2O
(10b) HOOSO2 − → SO3 − + OH
(11a) GQDs+ OH→GQDs + +OH−
(11b) GQDs+ SO3 − →GQDs
−
+SO3

The reaction of H2O2 and GQDs was also investigated in the absence and presence of bisulfite, which confirmed the occurrence of the mentioned processes. H2O2-GQDs reaction in alkaline condition caused a weak CL emission which could be relatively enhanced by addition of bisulfite (Fig. 5b). For the complimentary investigations, UV–vis absorption spectra of RB-H2O2 and RB-H2O2-GQDs-NaHSO3 CL systems were exhibited in Fig. S2a and S2b, respectively. Comparing the figures revealed that the decline in the absorption band of RB was occurred faster in the presence of GQDs. So, it can be considered that GQDs could facilitate the oxidation reaction of RB-H2O2. In other words, the reaction of GQDs with H2O2 generated more OH radicals facilitating the oxidation reaction of RB and resulted in the rapid consumption of RB. The results of UV–vis absorption spectra were accordance with those obtained from CL spectra, and it can be concluded that CL reaction in the RB-H2O2-GQDs-NaHSO3 system was fast due to application of GQDs and NaHSO3. It is mean that GQDs and NaHSO3 play as efficient catalysts in the oxidation of RB using H2O2.

Furthermore, it was found that HCHO had quenching effect on the RB-H2O2-GQDs-SDS-NaHSO3 CL system. It is worth to mention that HCHO can react with H2O2 [40,41] , thus, this reaction could decrease the possible associated CL reactions and weakened the CL signals. On the other hand, HCHO showed a very weak quenching effect on the fluorescence emission of RB or GQDs (Fig. S3). Consequently, it can be considered that the interaction of HCHO with GQDs or RB molecules are negligible and so, the main reason for the quenching effect of HCHO on CL emission is its reaction with H2O2.

The reducing effect of the HCHO on the emission intensity of RB-H2O2-GQDs-SDS-NaHSO3 CL system formed the basis for a sensitive chemosensor for HCHO determination (Fig. 7 ). The linear quenching response of the suggested CL procedure was obtained over the range of 0.02–11μgL−1 with linear equation as follows:
ΔI=8069C +3078.6 (R2 =0.9991)
Where ΔI=I0–Is and I0 and Is are the CL emission intensity of the introduced system in the absence or presence of HCHO and C represents HCHO concentration in μg L−1 (Fig. 7b). Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) were found to be 0.006 and 0.02μgL−1, respectively. In addition, the relative standard deviations (RSD%) for eleven replicated determinations of the solutions consisting 0.6 and 2.0μgL−1 of HCHO were obtained 2.93 and 1.85, respectively.

The aforementioned results confirmed that the introduced CL procedures exhibited high sensitivity, great analytical range and favorable precision. For the supplementary investigation, the analytical characteristics related to a number of published methods for HCHO monitoring, were compared with the introduced chemosensor in this study (Table 1 ). It is apparent that the introduced CL chemosensor possesses specific advantages such as simplicity, cost-effective instrumentation, suitable output, low detection limit and wide linear range.

Undoubtedly, identifying the selectivity of the procedure for HCHO in the presence of other foreign species is a key point. In order to estimate the possibility of the application of the introduced method for real samples, the responses of the CL reaction were monitored in the presence of 8.0μgL−1 HCHO and various quantities of the predicted interfering compounds. The changes more than 5% in CL responses could influence the determination of HCHO Therefore, the amount of compounds that induced relative error less than 5%, turned to tolerance point (Table 2 ). The results indicated no sensible interfering effect from the most of examined species. Just, some metal ions, such as Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ in high concentrations can relatively disturb determination method. So, the collected sample solutions were passed through a packed cation-exchanger column before their analysis to remove the interferences of metal ions.

A simple and sensitive CL probe was established for HCHO determination based on the catalytic effect of GQDs and sensitizing effect of HSO3 − on the RB-H2O2-SDS system. It was found that GQDs effectively promoted the weak CL emission of SDS-RB-H2O2 reaction. This promoting effect was improved in the presence of bisulfite. Consequently, an intense and efficient CL system was introduced based on RB- H2O2-SDS-GQDs-bisulfite assembly. Moreover, effect of HCHO, as a perilous compound, showed a specific quenching on the presented CL system. The decrease in CL intensity was proportional with HCHO in the wide linear range with a LOD of 0.006mgL−1. In summary, the HCHO concentration was satisfactorily assessed in real environmental water and wastewater samples using the introduced chemosensor in this study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the University of Tabriz for the support provided. We also acknowledge the support of Iran Science Elites Federation. This work is funded by the grant NRF-2015-002423 of the National Research Foundation of Korea.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.098.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Nanomaterial-amplified chemiluminescence systems and their applications in bioassays
Metal-nanoparticle-involved chemiluminescence and its applications in bioassays

Facile fabrication of light, flexible and multifunctional graphene fibers
Approaching ballistic transport in suspended graphene

Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives

Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers

Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots and its application to the determination of uric acid

Determination of formaldehyde in hair creams by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry

Flow-injection chemiluminescence determination of formaldehyde in water
Flow-injection spectrofluorometric determination of trace amounts of formaldehyde in water after derivatization with acetoacetanilide
A sensitive flow analysis system for the fluorimetric determination of low levels of formaldehyde in alcoholic beverages
A fluorescent bis-NBD derivative of calix [4] arene: switchable response to Ag+ and HCHO in solution phase
A novel lanthanide MOF thin film: the highly performance self-calibrating luminescent sensor for detecting formaldehyde as an illegal preservative in aquatic product
High performance formaldehyde detection based on a novel copper (II) complex functionalized QCM gas sensor
Novel reduced graphene oxide/Ag nanoparticle composite film with sensitive detection activity towards trace formaldehyde
Shaped-controlled electrosynthesis of gold nanodendrites for highly selective and sensitive SERS detection of formaldehyde
Kinetic spectrophotometric method for rapid determination of trace formaldehyde in foods
Determination of formaldehyde in foodstuffs by flow injection spectrophotometry using phloroglucinol as chromogenic agent
Determination of formaldehyde by flow injection analysis with spectrophotometric detection exploiting brilliant green–sulphite reaction
Ionic liquid-based dispersive liquid?liquid microextraction for the determination of formaldehyde in wastewaters and detergents
Detection of formaldehyde in food samples by enhanced chemiluminescence
Determination of formaldehyde based on the enhancement of the chemiluminescence produced by CdTe quantum dots and hydrogen peroxide
Continuous chemiluminescence determination of formaldehyde in air based on Trautz–Schorigin reaction
A flow-injection chemiluminescence determination of formaldehyde in textiles
A novel permanganate–morin–CdS quantum dots flow injection chemiluminescence system for sensitive determination of vancomycin
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots
Determination of cyanide using a chemiluminescence system composed of permanganate, rhodamine B, and gold nanoparticles
Flow-injection chemiluminescence analysis for sensitive determination of atenolol using cadmium sulfide quantum dots

Determination of copper in water by ionic liquid based microextraction and chemiluminescence detection
Hydroxyl radical from the reaction between hypochlorite and hydrogen peroxide

Generation of SO3- and OH radicals in SO32- reactions with inorganic environmental pollutants and its implications to SO32- toxicity
Kinetics of the oxidation of sulfite by hydrogen peroxide in acidic solution

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Alireza Khataee is currently a professor working in Department of Applied Chemistry at the University of Tabriz. He received his PhD in Applied Chemistry from University of Tabriz in 2007. He spent a sabbatical leave at Nancy University, Nancy, France. His research fields are chemiluminescence processes, advanced oxidation processes, and synthesis and characterization of nanomaterials. He has published 302 papers in international ISI indexed Journals, 21 papers in national Journals, 165 papers in international and national scientific conferences, 14 books/book chapters and 6 national patents.

Javad Hassanzadeh is currently a postdoctoral researcher in the laboratory of Prof. Alireza Khataee at the University of Tabriz. He received his PhD in Analytical Chemistry from University of Tabriz in 2013. He is interested in chemiluminescence & fluorescence reactions and flow analysis systems. He has published 24 papers in international ISI indexed Journals.

Roya Lotfi was an MSc student in the laboratory of Prof. Alireza Khataee at the University of Tabriz. She received his B.Sc. degree in Applied Chemistry from Faculty of Chemistry, Tabriz University in 2013. Her research interests focus on development of flow injection chemiluminescence systems based on nanomaterials properties for determination of pharmaceuticals.

Sang Woo Joo is currently a professor in World Class University Nano Research Center, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, South Korea. His research fields are nanomaterials, Lab on chip, biomedical engineering, and simulations.
